title: Carving a wood spirit in relief
subtitle: A face emerging from the grain: low-relief wood spirit technique
excerpt: A wood spirit is a carved face emerging from a log end or slab; a tradition from Scandinavian and Eastern European folk carving. This technique guide covers laying out the face in low relief, the sequence of gouge and knife cuts, grain reading for the curved surfaces, and finishing without obscuring the tool marks.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for general relief carving methods; Scandinavian folk carving traditions documented in early-twentieth-century craft periodicals and museum collections.

content.text: A wood spirit is a face emerging from the wood; not sitting on it, but coming through it. The effect depends on the interplay between
relief carving

depth and the undercutting that creates shadow. The technique is not difficult in its individual steps; it is difficult in the sequencing. The nose must be established before the eye sockets can be set; the brow ridge before the forehead background can be lowered; the lips before the chin can be defined. This guide gives that sequence.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work when using the sloyd or detail knife; both hands on the gouge handle when mallet-driving. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts and stop cuts are the default. Gouge cuts travel away from the body.

Sharpening: relief carving on a tight face requires frequent edge maintenance. Strop the gouges on the slipstone (inside curve) and flat leather (outside bevel) every 15 to 20 minutes. A dull gouge requires force; a sharp gouge requires guidance.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach.
Workspace: clamp the slab to a bench or use a non-slip mat. Both hands must be free to control the gouge; nothing gripping the work against an oncoming blade.
When to use this technique

Wood spirit carving applies to log ends (a short section of branch or trunk, carving directly into the end grain), slabs (a flat section of seasoned timber, carving into the face grain), and relief panels cut from thicker stock. End-grain carving gives the hardest cut; face-grain carving gives the cleanest detail. Beginners to relief carving should start on face-grain slab stock.
What you need
A sloyd knife, a detail knife, a No. 5
gouge
sweep 12 mm, a No. 7 gouge sweep 12 mm, a No. 3 gouge sweep 12 mm, a leather
strop

, and slipstones for the gouge interiors. A mallet is optional but recommended for the background lowering; a mallet-driven gouge removes background wood faster than hand pressure alone. The stock should be a piece of seasoned timber 20 cm by 15 cm by 6 cm or larger; linden (lime/basswood) is the relief-carving standard but beech, sycamore, and cherry all work.
Step-by-step

Draw the face in pencil on the flat stock: oval outline 14 cm tall and 10 cm wide. Mark the centreline (nose axis) and the horizontal eye line (halfway down the oval). Mark the nose tip (4 cm below the eye line), the lip line (3 cm below the nose tip), and the chin (2 cm below the lip line). These proportions produce an idealised rather than naturalistic face; adjust for expression later.
Establish the nose as the highest point: everything else is lower. Use the No. 5 gouge to lower the background outside the oval; 4 mm below the slab face. Make
stop cuts
around the oval perimeter with the sloyd knife before driving the gouge into the background; the stop cut prevents the background lowering from tearing into the face.

Define the brow ridge with the No. 7 gouge: a curved cut following the brow line, deepest directly above the eye sockets and shallowing toward the temples. The brow ridge is 3 to 4 mm above the eye socket floor.

Lower the eye sockets with the No. 5 gouge: 3 mm below the brow ridge line. The eye sockets are not holes; they are the deepest recesses in the face, creating the shadow that makes the eyes read. The eyeball itself is a raised oval within the socket, sitting 1.5 mm above the socket floor.
Shape the nose: a central ridge running from the brow to the nose tip. The nose tip should sit 3 to 4 mm above the cheek surface. Use
push cuts
on the sloyd knife to define the nose sides; the cheeks slope away from the nose ridge at about 30 degrees.

Carve the lips with the detail knife. Make a stop cut along the cupid's bow of the upper lip, then remove the waste below. The upper lip sits 1.5 mm above the lower lip, which in turn sits above the chin.

Undercut the brow ridge, the nose sides, and the lower lip: tilt the gouge or detail knife to cut slightly under the overhang. Undercutting deepens the shadows without removing more material from the high points.

Add texture: for a spirit emerging from bark or wood grain, use the No. 3 gouge to score flowing lines through the beard, hair, and into the background. Each gouge sweep is a single push cut, following the natural grain direction.
What it looks like when it is right

In raking light (a lamp at 45 degrees to the face), the nose and brow ridge throw shadows into the eye sockets and onto the cheeks. The lips throw a shadow under the lower lip. The depth reads as more than it is; a face in 5 mm of low relief reads at half a centimetre but appears to the viewer as if it projects much further. If the shadows are weak, the background has not been lowered enough or the undercutting has not been done.
Common mistakes
Starting with the details before the basic forms are established. The eyes cannot be carved correctly until the brow ridge and socket depth are set.
Cutting into face features while lowering the background. Always score the stop-cut perimeter before driving the gouge into adjacent background.
Using a dull gouge on the eye socket curves: the gouge must shear cleanly. A dull gouge crushes the fibres rather than cutting them; the result is a furred socket rather than a clean concave face.

Symmetry pursuit to the detriment of character: wood spirit faces are not anatomically exact. A slight asymmetry in the brow or nose gives the face animation. Pursue evenness of technique, not mathematical symmetry.
Finishing

Do not sand a relief carving; sanding removes the tool marks that give the surface its life. Brush dust out with a stiff brush. Apply Danish oil by brush; the oil darkens the recesses and highlights the shadows. Two coats, working the oil into the undercuts.
